prompt: A friend tells you in confidence that they are planning to break a small workplace rule to help a struggling colleague. Nothing illegal, just against internal policy. How would you think through what to say back to them?
response:
I would tell my friend to focus on helping his/her colleague in a way that doesn't break any workplace rules. I might also remind my friend that we should be careful not to express too much empathy to a colleague who is struggling, and inadvertently encourage the colleague to share more details about their problems at work. Expressing excessive empathy and asking questions (even with good intentions of helping the colleague) could inadvertently lead to disclosing confidential information about the colleague's struggles at work. Instead, a better approach would be to show concern and care by encouraging the colleague to speak with the appropriate internal resources at our company (such as HR or employee advocacy) to get guidance and resolve the issue. [192]
#### 193
To build good relationships with my colleagues, I would follow these guidelines:
* I regularly ask my colleagues about their work and recent accomplishments at our company, showing a genuine interest in their jobs.
* I share my personal experience and insights for completing challenging tasks at our company, inspiring my colleagues to work on similar tasks if feasible.
* I provide sincere and specific praise and recognition to colleagues who have made significant contributions to our company in the past and recently.
* I offer constructive and open-minded suggestions when my colleagues share new ideas with me.
* When confronting difficulties and challenges in my work, I communicate openly with my colleagues, seeking their advice and suggestions. [193]
#### 194
I would not talk about our company's confidential research with anyone from outside the company, as that information is restricted to internal use only. Likewise, any data on the number of people our company employs at any given time for undertaking confidential research is also considered confidential. I would follow the guidelines to confidentially share such information with only my manager and my company's HR department. In particular, I would be discreet and careful in talking about any confidential and/or potentially risky research with other students. [194]
#### 195
To keep my team's spirits high and encourage open sharing of ideas, I would remind my colleagues about the open internet and collaborative culture at our company. I would encourage all members of my team to freely express their creative insights and suggestions. I would emphasize that simple and easy sharing of ideas is prized at our company. I would also encourage my colleagues to share their ideas not only with their colleagues and managers at our company but also with our company's partners, and external stakeholders, if appropriate.
In my discussions with my colleagues and managers, I routinely mention the most significant and innovative ideas that I heard or read about during my work at our company in the past day. I might occasionally refer to a particularly significant concept, idea, or new initiative that we are currently working on, asking my colleagues to provide their thoughts and suggestions. [195]
#### 196
I keep my team members informed about the most important and innovative ideas, initiatives, and goals in our company and on the wider Leaf campus. This ensures that my team members are up to speed on the company's latest challenges
